operating_state: Total operating revenue increased 69% to $50.6 billion driven by continued recovery from COVID-19 pandemic impacts and higher refinery sales to third parties. System capacity expanded 20% as travel demand recovered, with premium products revenue benefiting from fleet mix improvements following 2020 retirements and new aircraft deliveries. Revenue increased 8% versus 2019 despite 15% lower capacity, with management planning 2023 system capacity to fully recover to or exceed 2019 levels.
cost_structure: Total operating expense increased 67% to $46.9 billion primarily from higher fuel costs due to both price increases and consumption growth from capacity restoration. Salaries and volume-related expenses rose with capacity expansion, while refinery sales expenses increased with third-party sales growth. Operating expense increases were amplified by the absence of $4.5 billion in payroll support program grants recognized in 2021.
material_events: Delta amended its revolving credit facility in November 2022, extending maturity on $1.325 billion of commitments from April 2023 to April 2025 while transitioning from LIBOR to SOFR reference rates. The Board approved bylaw amendments in December 2022 to address Rule 14a-19 stockholder nomination procedures and reflect Delaware General Corporation Law changes.
macro_exposures: EPA proposed regulations in 2022 defining PFAS as hazardous substances under CERCLA, potentially subjecting airports and airlines to cleanup liability for historical PFAS contamination from firefighting foams. ICAO established a more stringent CORSIA baseline of 85% of 2019 emissions applying 2024-2035, while EU legislation proposed expanding EU ETS reach and establishing sustainable aviation fuel mandates.
forward_outlook: Management issued 2023 guidance for $5-6 adjusted earnings per share with path to 2024 financial targets, while increasing December quarter outlook. The company expects system capacity to fully recover to or exceed 2019 levels in 2023 as pandemic recovery continues.
uncertainty_profile: Climate regulation costs remain unpredictable with CORSIA program details under development and potential political impacts from participating countries. Lower-carbon technology transition costs including SAF and direct air capture may prove prohibitively expensive without government incentives, while carbon offset costs have increased significantly as businesses announce sustainability goals.
